critique.summary: Sashiko is an open-source, agentic Linux kernel code review system designed to automate and enhance the review process of Linux kernel patches. It uses specialized prompts and a multi-stage review protocol to identify bugs and reinforce code quality, operating as a service funded by Google and maintained under the Linux Foundation.
mainPoints: Sashiko is inspired by the Japanese stitching technique and aims to reinforce the Linux kernel through automated patch review.
It monitors public mailing lists, particularly the Linux Kernel Mailing List (LKML), to evaluate proposed changes.
The system uses an open-source set of prompts and a custom multi-stage review protocol to improve accuracy and reduce false positives.
Sashiko is an open-source project under the Linux Foundation and licensed under Apache License 2.0.
Google funds the compute resources and LLM tokens used by Sashiko for automated reviews.
In tests with Gemini 3.1 Pro, Sashiko identified 53.6% of bugs from a sample of 1000 upstream commits with fixed bugs, all of which had passed human review previously.
The tool is probabilistic and intended to assist, not replace, human reviewers.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Sashiko align with publicly available information from the official Sashiko website and Linux Foundation announcements. The description of its functionality, open-source nature, and funding by Google is consistent with known facts. The performance metric of identifying 53.6% of bugs in tested commits is plausible given the current state of AI-assisted code review tools, and the caution about probabilistic output is a reasonable disclaimer. However, independent third-party verification of the bug detection rate is not provided in the article, and the reliance on a single LLM (Gemini 3.1 Pro) for testing may limit generalizability.
considerations: The article does not provide independent or third-party evaluations of Sashiko's effectiveness beyond internal testing.
Potential limitations or biases of the AI model (Gemini 3.1 Pro) used for reviews are not discussed.
The impact of Sashiko on the overall Linux kernel development workflow and acceptance by the developer community is not addressed.
Security and privacy implications of automated code review using LLMs are not mentioned.
The article does not compare Sashiko to other automated code review tools or human review processes.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the capabilities and limitations of the Sashiko Linux kernel code review system, particularly its handling of non-technical patch rejections and its bug detection accuracy compared to human reviewers. Participants highlight the system's usefulness in reducing reviewer fatigue despite some false positives.

comments.author: 4riel
comments.text: 53% catch rate on bugs that already cleared human review, with the main bottleneck being false positives... Sounds about right for where LLMs are useful: they don't get tired on patch 47 of a series
comments.analysis: This comment provides a data-driven insight into the system's performance and realistically assesses its strengths and limitations, making it the most accurate and constructive.

comments.author: ashwinnair99
comments.text: Curious how it handles the non-technical rejections. A lot of kernel patches get turned down for reasons that have nothing to do with the code itself.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid and thoughtful question about the system's ability to handle subjective or non-technical reasons for patch rejection, highlighting an important aspect often overlooked.

comments.author: rwmj
comments.analysis: This comment only provides a link to a previous discussion without adding new analysis or insight, making it the least substantive.
